ai_strategy_lead.name: Center for AI Responsibility and Research / AI for the Public Good
ai_strategy_lead.title: Public AI strategy or initiative lead / hub identified from cited sources

ai_strategy.summary: Binghamton University has a public AI posture anchored in Center for AI Responsibility and Research / AI for the Public Good. The cited sources show activity across responsible-use guidance, teaching resources, research or infrastructure, and AI literacy or workforce preparation.

key_provisions: Use AI transparently and follow course, research, workplace and academic-integrity expectations.
Do not enter confidential, regulated, sensitive, unpublished or protected institutional data into unapproved public AI tools.
Human users remain accountable for accuracy, bias, copyright, privacy and final decisions based on AI output.
ai_policy.summary: Binghamton University publishes AI policy or guidance with a comprehensive stance: AI use is allowed or actively explored, but bounded by privacy, ethics, academic integrity, compliance and human-review expectations.

governance_structure.summary: AI governance at Binghamton University is evidenced through Center for AI Responsibility and Research / AI for the Public Good and the cited policy or strategy sources. Public sources did not identify a separate chief AI officer during this sweep.

narrative_summary: Binghamton University has a documented AI posture across policy, teaching, research, infrastructure or access. The central public signal is Center for AI Responsibility and Research / AI for the Public Good, supported by policy or guidance sources that encourage responsible use while emphasizing privacy, academic integrity, ethics and human oversight. The institution also shows curriculum or AI literacy activity and at least one research, center, infrastructure or applied innovation signal. Its maturity is strongest where public sources show durable structures: committees, centers, portals, degree programs, supercomputing, enterprise tools, or workforce initiatives. The gaps are also important: this sweep did not find a standalone chief AI officer in public sources for most assigned institutions, and several initiatives do not disclose exact budgets, user counts or implementation metrics. Overall, the evidence supports a medium-confidence R1 profile with enough public signals to compare the institution across governance, teaching, research, enterprise tools, workforce and equity dimensions.

research_quality.compiled_by: codex / collect-institution skill / Worker 1 gap sweep
search_queries: site:binghamton.edu Binghamton Microsoft Copilot Gemini NotebookLM AI tools students faculty
Binghamton largest academic gift AI center 55 million
site:binghamton.edu Binghamton University AI assessment redesign generative AI syllabus ChatGPT teaching center
gaps_acknowledged: Public sources did not identify a standalone chief AI officer role during this sweep.
Some AI initiatives did not disclose dollar amounts, user counts, or detailed implementation metrics.
Leadership names were not populated because the assigned task prioritized missing AI posture profiles and every non-obvious AI claim is URL-cited.
Follow-up search confirmed authenticated AI tool access and a $55M responsible-AI center commitment, but no public user counts or per-tool contract values were disclosed.
Follow-up search did not locate a Binghamton-local GPU count; public compute-capacity evidence remains limited to AI/ML research and broader Empire AI context.

strategic_archetype.rationale: Primary archetype selected from visible public signals: governance led.
